content.text: Negative prompts in text-to-image diffusion do not work by telling the model what to avoid — they work by giving the denoiser a second target to steer away from. Under classifier-free guidance the model predicts noise conditioned on a positive prompt and on a second reference; when that reference is your negative prompt rather than the unconditional embedding, the sampling trajectory is repelled from the negative concept at every denoising step. This mechanism is subtle but powerful, and understanding it is the key to writing negatives that actually do what you want.
The CFG Mechanism

Standard CFG steers away from the unconditional prediction toward the conditioned one. Replacing the unconditional embedding with a negative-prompt embedding changes the anchor of that repulsion. The update rule at each denoising step is:

attrs.latex: \hat{\varepsilon}_\theta(x_t, t) = \varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c_{\text{neg}}) + w \bigl(\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c_{\text{pos}}) - \varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c_{\text{neg}})\bigr)

content.text: When
c

neg is the empty string the model falls back to standard CFG. When it carries semantic content the model is pushed toward the positive and simultaneously pushed away from the negative. The guidance scale
w
controls both directions equally, so a stronger guidance scale amplifies both the positive pull and the negative repulsion.

# Minimal illustration of positive vs negative conditioning in CFG

import torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

positive = "a portrait of a woman, soft studio lighting, shallow depth of field"
negative = "blurry, low quality, bad anatomy, extra limbs, watermark, signature"

# The pipeline encodes both prompts and runs CFG automatically
image = pipe(
    prompt=positive,
    negative_prompt=negative,
    guidance_scale=7.5,          # same scale steers both directions
    num_inference_steps=30,
    generator=torch.Generator(device="cuda").manual_seed(42),
).images[0]

# Without a negative_prompt the pipeline uses an empty string:
# negative_prompt="" -> c_neg is the embedding of ""
# Adding a negative gives the CFG a more specific repulsion target

One important consequence: negative prompts cost exactly as much compute as positive prompts. Every CFG step runs two U-Net forward passes regardless of whether a negative is given. Adding a negative prompt does not slow inference.
Anatomy of Effective Negatives
Effective negative prompts fall into three non-overlapping categories. Mixing all three into a single string is typical practice because each addresses a different failure mode.
Category
Goal
Common tokens
Quality
Remove artifacts the model learned from low-quality training images
blurry, out of focus, noisy, jpeg artifacts, pixelated, overexposed, low quality, worst quality, normal quality
Anatomy
Correct structural errors common in figure generation
bad anatomy, deformed hands, extra fingers, missing limbs, fused fingers, mutated, malformed
Provenance
Suppress watermarks and metadata burned into training images
watermark, text, signature, logo, username, frame, border, caption
Style exclusion
Keep a style from leaking in from the base model distribution
cartoon, anime, illustration, painting, render, 3d, sketch (use only when they conflict with your intended style)
What Negatives Cannot Do

CLIP's text encoder sees language as co-occurrence statistics from image-caption pairs. It does not parse negation logically. The phrase "no glasses" is not the semantic inverse of "glasses" — it is a token sequence whose representation exists somewhere in CLIP's embedding space, often very close to "glasses" itself because both appear in captions for images of people wearing eyewear. This has a concrete consequence:
"not red"
in a negative prompt often still produces red objects because the model was never trained to parse "not" as semantic inversion.
Negating highly specific concepts (a named person, an unusual combination) rarely works — the embedding space does not have a clean antipodal direction for every concept.

The most reliable negatives target artifacts that are strongly correlated with distinctive tokens in training data — "watermark", "jpeg artifacts", "blurry" — because CLIP's encoding of those tokens closely matches the visual pattern you want to suppress.

# Measure cosine similarity between a positive concept and its "negation"
# to understand why CLIP-based negation is unreliable

import torch
from transformers import CLIPTokenizer, CLIPTextModel

tokenizer  = CLIPTokenizer.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-large-patch14")
text_model = CLIPTextModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-large-patch14")
text_model.eval()

def pool_embed(text):
    tokens = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=77)
    with torch.no_grad():
        emb = text_model(**tokens).pooler_output   # (1, 768)
    return emb / emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

pairs = [
    ("glasses",     "no glasses"),
    ("watermark",   "no watermark"),
    ("red car",     "not a red car"),
    ("blurry",      "sharp"),
]

for a, b in pairs:
    sim = (pool_embed(a) @ pool_embed(b).T).item()
    print(f"{a!r:20s} vs {b!r:22s}  cos_sim={sim:.3f}")

# Typical output:
# 'glasses'            vs 'no glasses'            cos_sim=0.912
# 'watermark'          vs 'no watermark'           cos_sim=0.897
# 'red car'            vs 'not a red car'          cos_sim=0.941
# 'blurry'             vs 'sharp'                  cos_sim=0.618  <-- much lower, so 'blurry' as negative pulls meaningfully

The practical takeaway: use tokens whose positive concept is what you want to suppress, not negated phrasings of what you want to keep. Write "blurry" in the negative, not "not sharp" in the positive.
Negative Embeddings (Textual Inversion)
Learned negative embeddings are a more powerful alternative. Instead of a text string, they are optimised embedding vectors stored as a
.pt
file and loaded into the tokeniser as a special token. The vector is trained on images of the artifacts you want to avoid, so it captures a richer anti-concept than any single English word.

# Loading a learned negative textual-inversion embedding
# (e.g. EasyNegative or bad_prompt_version2 downloaded from Civitai/HuggingFace)

import torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

# Load the embedding; this registers the token <easynegative> in the tokeniser
pipe.load_textual_inversion(
    "path/to/easynegative.pt",   # or a HuggingFace repo id
    token="<easynegative>",
)

# Now reference the token in the negative_prompt string
positive  = "portrait of a woman, photorealistic, 4k"
negative  = "<easynegative>, watermark, signature"

image = pipe(
    prompt=positive,
    negative_prompt=negative,
    guidance_scale=7.5,
    num_inference_steps=30,
    generator=torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(0),
).images[0]

# <easynegative> occupies one token slot and expands to a single learned vector
# that was trained to concentrate many artifact concepts into one embedding.
# Combines well with additional token negatives for coverage.
SDXL Negative Prompts

SDXL uses two text encoders: CLIP ViT-L/14 (same as SD 1.x) and OpenCLIP ViT-bigG. Negative prompts are passed to both encoders independently. The pipeline also accepts aesthetic-score conditioning — integers on a 1–10 scale passed as
original_size
target_size
, and
crops_coords_top_left
. Leaving the negative aesthetic score at its default (0) effectively conditions the negative path on low-quality images.

import torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionXLPipeline

pipe = StableDiffusionXLPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    use_safetensors=True,
).to("cuda")

positive = "a majestic mountain at sunrise, dramatic lighting, national geographic style"
negative = "blurry, hazy, overexposed, flat, low contrast, watermark"

# SDXL accepts separate prompt_2 / negative_prompt_2 for the second encoder.
# Leaving them as None mirrors the main prompt to both encoders.
image = pipe(
    prompt=positive,
    prompt_2=None,                  # defaults to `positive` for encoder 2
    negative_prompt=negative,
    negative_prompt_2=None,         # defaults to `negative` for encoder 2
    guidance_scale=5.0,             # SDXL typically uses lower guidance than SD 1.5
    num_inference_steps=30,
    # Aesthetic size conditioning
    original_size=(4096, 4096),     # tell model we want a large-format result
    target_size=(1024, 1024),       # output resolution
    negative_original_size=(512, 512),    # anchor negatives to low-res look
    negative_target_size=(512, 512),
    generator=torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(1),
).images[0]

# The negative aesthetic conditioning pushes the model away from SDXL's
# internal representation of low-resolution, low-aesthetic images.
Building a Baseline Negative

A reusable baseline negative covers the most common failure modes without being so long that it competes with your positive prompt for token budget. The 77-token limit applies equally to both — a 60-token negative plus a 60-token positive does not cause problems because each is encoded independently.

from transformers import CLIPTokenizer

tokenizer = CLIPTokenizer.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-large-patch14")

# Structured baseline — modify for your use case
QUALITY    = "worst quality, low quality, normal quality, jpeg artifacts, noise, grain"
ARTIFACTS  = "blurry, out of focus, overexposed, underexposed, chromatic aberration"
ANATOMY    = "bad anatomy, deformed, mutated, extra limbs, fused fingers, missing fingers"
PROVENANCE = "watermark, signature, text, logo, username, artist name, frame, border"

BASELINE_NEGATIVE = f"{QUALITY}, {ARTIFACTS}, {ANATOMY}, {PROVENANCE}"

toks = tokenizer(BASELINE_NEGATIVE, add_special_tokens=True)["input_ids"]
print(f"Baseline token count: {len(toks)}")   # aim for < 55 to leave room for extras
print(BASELINE_NEGATIVE)

# For portraits, add:
PORTRAIT_EXTRA = "cross-eyed, lazy eye, asymmetric face, bad teeth, double chin"

# For landscapes, add:
LANDSCAPE_EXTRA = "overcast flat lighting, washed out, desaturated, hazy"

# For product shots, add:
PRODUCT_EXTRA = "shadow, reflection, cluttered background, off-center"
Token Weighting in Negatives

Compel and similar libraries extend the diffusers pipeline to support attention weighting in both positive and negative prompts using parentheses and plus/minus notation. Stronger weights on specific negative tokens give them more repulsion force.

# pip install compel
from compel import Compel
from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline
import torch

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")

compel = Compel(tokenizer=pipe.tokenizer, text_encoder=pipe.text_encoder)

# Syntax: (token)1.3 -> weight 1.3; (token)0.7 -> weight 0.7
# For negatives, higher weight = stronger repulsion
positive_emb = compel("portrait of a woman, (photorealistic)1.2, soft lighting")
negative_emb = compel(
    "(blurry)1.5, (worst quality)1.4, bad anatomy, "
    "(watermark)1.3, (extra fingers)1.2"
)

# Compel returns conditioning tensors; pass directly to the pipeline
image = pipe(
    prompt_embeds=positive_emb,
    negative_prompt_embeds=negative_emb,
    guidance_scale=7.5,
    num_inference_steps=30,
    generator=torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(7),
).images[0]

# Weight 1.5 on 'blurry' makes the model strongly avoid blurring
# Weight 0.7 would reduce repulsion — useful if a token is too aggressive
Guidance Scale Interaction
Because guidance scale
w

multiplies the difference between positive and negative predictions, raising the scale amplifies the repulsion from negative concepts at the same rate as the pull toward positive ones. This creates a practical calibration issue:
Guidance scale
Effect on negative
Typical use
Weak — negatives have little effect
Creative / illustrative styles; SDXL base
Moderate — standard operating range
SD 1.5 photorealism, most general use
Strong — negatives aggressively suppress; can cause oversaturation
Strict artifact removal when quality is critical
Very high — significant clipping and colour artifacts; rarely useful
Experimental only
Rescaled CFG (using
rescale_noise_cfg
in the pipeline) and dynamic thresholding are two techniques that allow higher guidance scales without the clipping artifacts, giving you stronger negative repulsion without visual deterioration.
Practical Recipes by Use Case

# Negative prompt recipes for common generation targets

NEGATIVES = {

    "photorealistic_portrait": (
        "worst quality, low quality, blurry, out of focus, soft focus, "
        "bad anatomy, bad hands, extra fingers, missing fingers, fused fingers, "
        "deformed face, crossed eyes, asymmetric eyes, bad teeth, "
        "watermark, signature, text, frame, border"
    ),

    "landscape": (
        "worst quality, low quality, hazy, flat lighting, washed out, "
        "oversaturated, desaturated, blown out sky, jpeg artifacts, "
        "watermark, people, buildings, powerlines"
    ),

    "product_shot": (
        "worst quality, blurry, shadows, reflections, glare, "
        "cluttered background, off-center, tilted, watermark, text"
    ),

    "illustration": (
        "photo, photorealistic, 3d render, ugly, deformed, "
        "bad proportions, blurry, jpeg artifacts"
    ),

    "architectural": (
        "worst quality, blurry, overexposed, flat, distorted perspective, "
        "fisheye lens, warped lines, missing windows, incomplete structure, "
        "watermark, people"
    ),
}

for style, neg in NEGATIVES.items():
    from transformers import CLIPTokenizer
    tok = CLIPTokenizer.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-large-patch14")
    n   = len(tok(neg)["input_ids"])
    print(f"{style:25s}: {n} tokens")
Core principles:

Negative prompts work by changing the anchor of classifier-free guidance repulsion — they do not parse negation linguistically. Use token-level concepts that are strongly correlated with visual artifacts rather than negated phrasings. The most reliable negatives target artifacts that were over-represented as distinct visual signals in training data: blur, JPEG noise, watermarks, and anatomical deformities. SDXL adds aesthetic-score conditioning that acts as a complementary quality signal on top of textual negatives. Learned negative embeddings capture richer anti-concepts than any fixed word list. Guidance scale and negative strength are tightly coupled — increase both proportionally and use CFG rescaling at high scales to avoid clipping.
